commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: structured ClawSweeper needs-human verdict with repairable P-severity findings (sha=800dbdad3d2ec86da0bc17081eea98b2f488411e)
commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a `clearInterval(renewInterval)` guard in `startGmailWatcher` and fake-timer regression tests for repeated starts creating only one renewal callback.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main overwrites `renewInterval` without clearing the previous handle, so repeated successful starts can leave an unreachable timer. I did not run tests because this review is read-only.
commands.review_followup: No automated repair is queued because the PR still needs contributor-supplied real behavior proof, plus a maintainer decision on fixing the re-entry process/timer ownership before merge.

target.title: fix(hooks): clear Gmail watcher renewal interval on re-entry
target.body:
## Problem

`startGmailWatcher()` in `src/hooks/gmail-watcher.ts` creates a `setInterval` for Gmail watch renewal but overwrites `renewInterval` without clearing the previous interval first. If `startGmailWatcher` is called more than once (e.g. during a configuration reload), each call leaks the old interval timer. The leaked timers keep firing `startGmailWatch()` on their own schedule, causing duplicate renewal calls and preventing the watcher from ever being fully stopped.

The `stopGmailWatcher` function already clears `renewInterval` correctly (line 313), but the start function path was missing the same guard.

## Fix

Add a `clearInterval(renewInterval)` guard immediately before the `setInterval` call in `startGmailWatcher` (line 287). If a renewal interval already exists from a previous start, it is cleared before creating a new one. This matches the existing pattern in `stopGmailWatcher`.

Production diff is +4 lines changed in 1 file (`gmail-watcher.ts`), plus 46 lines of test coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Calling `startGmailWatcher()` more than once leaks the previous `setInterval` timer. The leaked interval keeps firing `startGmailWatch()` renewal calls indefinitely, even after `stopGmailWatcher()` clears only the latest interval reference.

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source (commit 1d5b5db) at `/tmp/fix-82947`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-82947
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Verify clearInterval guard in compiled production code
grep -n -B2 -A2 "clearInterval(renewInterval)" dist/gmail-watcher-CcMBKtwy.js

# 3. Start the patched gateway (gmail-watcher module loads and stops cleanly)
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway

# 4. Run Gmail watcher test suite
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Compiled production code verification

Both `clearInterval(renewInterval)` guards are present in the compiled Gmail watcher bundle:

```console
$ grep -n -B2 -A2 "clearInterval(renewInterval)" dist/gmail-watcher-CcMBKtwy.js
196-watcherProcess = spawnGogServe(runtimeConfig);
197-if (renewInterval) {
198:clearInterval(renewInterval);
199-renewInterval = null;
200-}
--
213-shuttingDown = true;
214-if (renewInterval) {
215:clearInterval(renewInterval);
216-renewInterval = null;
217-}
```

Line 198 is the new guard added by this PR (clear before creating a new interval in `startGmailWatcher`). Line 215 is the existing guard in `stopGmailWatcher`. Before this fix, only the stop path cleared the interval; the start path overwrote `renewInterval` without clearing the old timer, leaking it.

### Live gateway startup proof

The patched gateway starts, loads the gmail-watcher module, and shuts down cleanly:

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T12:44:07.384-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T12:44:07.440-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T12:44:09.521-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T12:44:10.377-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T12:44:10.378-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, device-pair, file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 2.9s)
2026-05-21T12:44:10.743-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T12:44:30.863-07:00 [gateway] signal SIGTERM received
2026-05-21T12:44:30.866-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T12:44:30.913-07:00 [gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped
2026-05-21T12:44:30.914-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 39ms
```

The `[gmail-watcher] gmail watcher stopped` line confirms the watcher module is loaded and participates in graceful shutdown. The clearInterval guard ensures that if `startGmailWatcher` were called again before shutdown, the old interval would be properly cleared.

### Vitest test suite (5 tests, supplemental)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts
 ✓ |hooks| ../../src/hooks/gmail-watcher.test.ts (5 tests) 155ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
```

The test suite includes a focused regression test for the interval leak: calls `startGmailWatcher` twice and verifies `clearInterval` was called on the first interval before creating the second.

Observed result after fix: The compiled production bundle contains the new `clearInterval(renewInterval)` guard at line 198 (start path), matching the existing guard at line 215 (stop path). The gateway starts cleanly with the patched gmail-watcher module. Before the fix, only `stopGmailWatcher` cleared the interval; `startGmailWatcher` overwrote the reference without clearing. All 5 tests pass.

What was not tested: Triggering an actual configuration reload that calls `startGmailWatcher` twice during a live gateway session. The compiled code verification and regression test confirm the guard is in place and fires correctly.
target.author: sebtardif

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
